data.title: Cal-Maine Foods: Amazing Financials And Ready For A Turnaround
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Cal-Maine Foods (NASDAQ:CALM) is currently the number one US fresh egg producer, with 43.8 million laying hens and 1.3 billion dozen eggs sold annually as of FY2025. The company is currently in an amazing financial position and is looking into ways to diversify its portfolio and rely less on the volatility of eggs. However, as the stock is following egg prices and is up about 48% since a year ago on the back of eggs breaking record levels in recent history, a few months ago, let’s see how sustainable this valuation is.

We see an overall stable production in recent years as the company adapted to the environment and slowly expanded itself into the largest fresh egg producer in the US. The company has a market share of roughly 24%, based on its latest estimates.

They have a diverse set of clients, including Walmart (WMT), Costco (COST), and Food Lion (OTCQX:ADRNY), which also ties them to the economy. Still, eggs are an essential, high-protein food that remains affordable throughout the cycle and versatile, often being backed by solid demand despite the prices.

The stock has been quite volatile in the years until 2024, when the company basically doubled on account of significant volatility in the egg market, following the price of eggs quite closely. Still, CALM has a history of solid performance throughout the cycle, but the company’s actions may help solidify this into a less cycle-dependent business, as I’ll mention below. This is the result of their investments and acquisitions, although for now, egg prices will remain the key factor in determining their performance.

We can see a very significant jump in cash flows back in 2023 and especially in the past fiscal year, backed by a major jump in the price of eggs. Like any other commodity company, their numbers improved significantly during the upside, and we recently saw the stock rally after the higher prices boosted their latest earnings. I’ll talk more about this below, but keep in mind that this cycle is normal for commodities, including eggs. To get an idea, the average selling price for conventional eggs in the latest quarter was $3.78 per dozen vs. $2.06 a year ago, so nearly a 2x in price, for reasons I’ll also mention below.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see that Cal-Maine is in an excellent financial position, with the current assets covering the TOTAL liabilities nearly 4 times! Their $499.4 million in cash is almost enough to cover the total liabilities, and the company has zero debt, meaning they should prove to be very resilient and won’t have to worry about the price of eggs even during the downside, being a financial fortress that can withstand many years of losses before it would feel a significant negative effect (it can take several years of downcycle to burn through all their cash/securities). Still, there’s $2.56 billion in equity on a ~$5.13 billion market cap, placing the PB at ~2.

The company currently has $1.39 billion in net cash, but an important thing to mention here is that this wasn’t always the case. Yes, the company was always in a great position with net cash, but back in 2022, for example, they only had $174.5 million in net cash, which is a very important part of the valuation. It will be very tough to see them lose this amazing financial position, and at “worst”, I’d expect them to do significant acquisitions, which should be accretive for the cash flow, so it's still good for them in the long term regardless.

Dividends & Buybacks

The dividend currently stands at a very significant yield since the company is rewarding investors following the boom in egg prices that resulted in record levels of cash flows and net income. However, as prices revert to their mean, we should not expect this type of dividend forever. As seen several times in the past 10 years, the company can even pay zero dividends, so don’t expect a stable dividend from a commodity producer.

They allocate a third of the net income under their variable dividend policy, which is why the last few dividends have been so significant. It might seem like much, but the past 5 years would represent an average of ~3.13% from the current price, and that’s despite 2 of their highest dividends ever. This is why buying any commodity company during high prices is generally bad—with few exceptions—as even the great times would come with limited results compared to buying during the downside, which can result in significant double-digit yields during the upside, on top of potential multi-baggers.

Risks & Opportunities

For now, keep in mind that the company will depend on egg prices. They saw nice numbers over the past years, backed by significantly above-average egg prices, but we will most likely see them revert over the following years.

Thanks to the higher egg prices, the company saw an amazing FY2023 and especially FY2025, despite it reverting and the cash flow then falling by ~50% in 2024. They are using these cash flows to expand into less volatile sectors, but the company’s earnings will likely continue to depend significantly on egg prices in the near future. Still, we see several acquisitions made by Cal-Maine over the past years, such as probably their most ambitious investment yet (Echo Lake Foods earlier this year) for $258 million, expanding their offering in the ready-to-eat egg products and breakfast foods market (i.e., waffles, pancakes, pre-cooked omelets).

This type of acquisition can help stabilize the company’s revenue to some degree, as food prices will not present the same volatility as an individual commodity, while the company is able to integrate this into its model and likely deliver significant synergies since it's supplying its own eggs. This comes on top of their 51% stake in Crepini, a producer of similar egg-based prepared foods, as well as the expansion of their MeadowCreek Foods, a hard-boiled egg producer. Overall, we can see them vertically integrating into more of a food company rather than just a commodity producer, which could provide more stability and predictability, which the market should appreciate and even trade at better multiples, especially if they continue this trend.

As mentioned above, the price of eggs has nearly doubled since a year ago, and the company provided a very clear reason as to why:

The higher market prices were a direct result of the reduced supply of shell eggs across the industry due to outbreaks of highly pathogenic avian influenza (HPAI) during a period of high demand for eggs and egg products around the Easter holiday.

The USDA announced up to $1 billion worth of investments in the industry to help fight HPAI, so we are likely to see this normalize eventually. So, we’re looking at a commodity whose price has been boosted by a mix of natural/environmental causes at a time when egg demand was at its highest—Easter. In a way, this was a “perfect storm” for Cal-Maine, and it’s not impossible to see this happen in the future as well. Having worked with specialists in the industry some years ago, I’ve heard stories of how even the most careful farms that paid very significant attention to healthcare in an effort of fighting some disease that was common at the time failed, with a single mistake leading to the farm being forced to wipe out the entire herd, resulting in very significant losses. This is unfortunately common for the industry, and we are likely to see this happen from time to time, adding to the cyclicality of the prices. High prices will then attract more production, which should be visible relatively quickly in the market since this industry, unlike mining, has much faster capital turnover than mining, for example, which requires even a decade from exploration to permitting, financing, developing, and mining.

The stock is up nearly 60% over the past year, backed by the higher price of eggs and the company’s results, but we also have to note the significant ~11.23% short interest. This means that there is some potential for a short squeeze, which recently made the stock jump on the back of the WallStreetBets hype, together with many other highly-shorted stocks. This short interest likely comes as a result of the higher prices, which are expected to be only temporary due to the reasons mentioned above, which won’t last forever. As egg prices normalize, the stock should follow to some degree, especially in the short and medium-term, until their investments lead to a weaker connection between the company and egg prices. This could also lead to better multiples, as they could shift from a pure-play commodity company to a mixture of commodities and food, the latter potentially adding more stability.

Peer Comparison

For a peer comparison, I’ll go with a mixture of direct competitors such as Vital Farms (VITL), but also other companies in the packaged foods and meats market, such as Post Holdings (POST), Tyson Foods (TSN), J.M. Smucker (SJM), Hormel Foods (HRL), and Conagra Brands (CAG), to get a broader idea about the industry, including prepared meals and other food products.

Compared to Vital Farms, we see that Cal-Maine is significantly undervalued if we look at any valuation metrics, including the PB (which doesn’t have much to do with egg prices). Still, even compared to the broader industry, CALM seems to be cheaper than all the other companies. However, what I wanted to point out with this comparison is the kind of ratios we can expect. Right now, CALM is so low, especially because of the very high egg prices. Have these numbers fall in half when egg prices normalize, and we are suddenly at similar numbers to these peers—nothing exceptional. So, in reality, CALM is not really undervalued if we adjust for a normal/average price throughout the cycle.

Valuation

As for a DCF valuation of Cal-Maine, I believe $175 million should be a fair average FCF to expect throughout a normal cycle, especially backed by their investments and inflation, assuming they cut the CAPEX during bad times from the recent all-time highs we’ve seen lately. This FCF is still projected to grow at a 4% CAGR for the first 5 years and then at a 3% CAGR for the other 5 years (in line with long-term egg demand expectations). With a 2% terminal growth rate and a WACC of 6.5%—in line with market estimates—and considering a very significant $1.39 billion in net cash along with 48.5 million shares outstanding, the resulting DCF model yields an enterprise value of approximately $4.33 billion. After adding the net cash, this leads to an equity value of roughly $5.72 billion, or an intrinsic value per share of about $117.86.

That’s slightly above the current price, and although it may seem like much given the past stock performance, I’ll mention again that their financials changed significantly. Their significant net cash position will be hard to burn through, and if the management plays it right, it can turn out to be very accretive for their free cash flows, potentially opening the way for significant expansion—in or outside the US, vertically or horizontally.

Conclusions

Overall, Cal-Maine seems to be in a position where it can really transform the business from a volatile commodity producer into one with a more stable business model, which could potentially attract and support a higher valuation for longer. Their excellent financial position and net cash represent a big advantage, allowing the management to reshape the company for a better future. I rate Cal-Maine a Buy now for their long-term potential, but keep in mind that we are talking about relying quite a bit on the management to make the right moves in a volatile environment, so don’t think this is a risk-free investment.

data.title: Civeo: Management's Transformative Acquisition Makes Shares Even Cheaper
data.description:
In my opinion, one of the most interesting companies on the market has got to be Civeo Corporation (NYSE:CVEO). This is because it is such a unique firm. For those not familiar with it, it essentially provides accommodation services for the natural resource extraction industries in Canada and Australia. It provides temporary housing for those working in remote areas, as well as services associated with that housing. Financial performance recently, and in fact for the last few years now, has been a bit disappointing. Even so, shares of the business trade at rock bottom levels. This was why, in February of this year, I reaffirmed the company as a ‘strong buy’ candidate. Since then, shares have risen by 7.5%, outpacing the 5.6% increase achieved by the S&P 500 over the same window of time.

Honestly, I believe that continued outperformance is justified. This is especially true after management made a big acquisition and landed a large contract that should create additional revenue and cash flows moving forward. I don't like the fact that this required a pretty sizable increase in net debt after management had worked so hard to get that down. But in the long run, I think it will all be worth it. Of course, as new data comes out, investors would be wise to incorporate that into the picture. And it just so happens that, before the market opens on July 29th, management will be reporting financial results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, they expect continued weakness. But that's not enough to change my overall assessment at this time.

A look at recent results

Fundamentally speaking, things have not been particularly great for Civeo Corporation. To understand what I mean, we need only look at the results for the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year compared to the same time last year. During that time, revenue for the company contracted from $166.1 million to $144 million. To understand this, it would be best to dig into each of its operating segments.

The problem child for the company ended up being its Canadian segment. According to management, revenues associated with that part of the company fell sharply from $67.2 million to $40.4 million. This came as the number of billed rooms plummeted from 610,032 to 358,697. Management blamed this on the fact that oil sands operators have decided to prioritize reducing operating costs as opposed to maximizing output. The firm saw reduced occupancy at its Sitka Lodge as well as the Kitimat LNG facility, which is located nearest to completion. And there ended up being reduced mobile asset activity from pipeline projects that were completed early last year. It is worth mentioning that the average daily rate for lodges also dropped, declining from $98 to $93. This is a reflection of overall market weakness. Honestly, I don't find this to be surprising in the least bit. WCS crude in the first quarter of 2025, for instance, came in at $58.27 per barrel. Although that is only marginally lower than the $59.48 per barrel reported the previous year, over the same window of time, WTI crude prices dropped 7.3% in light of broader economic concerns. Overall weakness in the energy industry is bound to have a deleterious effect on companies such as this.

As painful as operations in Canada ended up being, the exact opposite was true in Australia. Revenue there actually jumped from $91.7 million to $103.6 million. This was in spite of the fact that the average daily rate for villages contracted from $77 to $75. Meanwhile, the firm saw a rise in the number of billed rooms from 613,936 to 625,636. Even with this improvement, however, total accommodation revenue contracted. The real driver here was on the food service and other services revenue side of things, which skyrocketed from $44.6 million to $56.8 million. This is because of new business in its integrated services villages in Western Australia. This stemmed from a six-year contract that the company won that should bring with it around A$1.4 billion in revenue during its lifetime.

The drop in revenue for the company brought with it a decline in profitability, also. Net profits went from negative $5.1 million to negative $9.8 million. This was in spite of the fact that the company booked a $7.8 million impairment charge in the first quarter of last year and was driven in part by a $6.1 million gain on the sale of its McClelland Lake Lodge assets. Certain expenses, such as selling, general, and administrative costs, ended up being sticky year over year. The same applied to depreciation and amortization costs. Other profitability metrics also worsened. Operating cash flow went from $6 million to negative $8.4 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $12.3 million to $6.3 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company pulled back from $17.8 million to $12.7 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2024 fiscal year compared to 2023. This shows continued weakness, driven almost entirely by the firm's Canadian operations. In the image below, you can see a breakdown of key metrics that shows a reduction in activity in Canada, even as operations in Australia picked up. Naturally, this had a negative impact on the bottom line for the company, also, with profits and cash flows dropping year over year.

For the current fiscal year in its entirety, management has provided mixed guidance. Overall revenue is now expected to be between $620 million and $650 million. Even at the high end, that would represent another year-over-year decline. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company is anticipated to be between $75 million and $85 million. The midpoint there would almost exactly match what the company saw last year. So at least in that regard, things are looking more solid.

It is vital that we consider the fact that this guidance does not factor in the four villages that management announced the acquisition of in February of this year. These are based in the Australian Bowen Basin and will cost shareholders a whopping $67 million. As the chart below illustrates, this will increase the net debt picture of the company rather significantly. The good news, however, is that the transaction does make shares cheaper. This is because the overall annual revenue associated with this business is estimated to be around $32 million. From that, management expects to extract EBITDA of approximately $17 million a year.

This purchase price implies an EV to EBITDA multiple of about 3.9. This is lower than the 5.6 multiple that Civeo Corporation is trading at without factoring in the acquisition. Management does have an Australian revolving credit facility that has an annualized interest rate of about 6.8%. Using the assumption that management will utilize that for the purchase, it is possible to not only look at the pro forma EBITDA for the company but also to look at the pro forma operating cash flow.

Using these assumptions, I was able to show in the chart above how shares of the company are priced using pro forma figures for 2024 and pro forma estimates for 2025, and also to look at how this stacks up against the company under the assumption that the transaction would never be completed. As you can see, the stock is certainly cheaper as a result of this purchase. But the downside to this is that overall net leverage is expected to rise. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean. This is naturally unfortunate to see. But considering the lower trading multiple, I don't have much of an issue.

There are some other reasons to be optimistic. In May of this year, management announced that it was awarded a three-year contract with a major metallurgical coal producer in Australia to provide integrated services at two villages in the Bowen Basin. This arrangement, which will be completed over three years, should generate around $40.1 million in revenue for the business. However, this was factored into the recent guidance provided by management. The firm also received, in June of this year, a four-year contract renewal to provide rooms and hospitality services in that same area in a deal valued at $159.5 million over the span of four years. Although this was also incorporated into the guidance for the year, it is a sign that the future for the company should pick up.

It will be interesting to see the extent to which these contracts could affect backlog moving forward. In the chart above, you can see that, at the end of the most recent quarter, Civeo Corporation had a backlog of $519.1 million. That is down from the $564.8 million it had at the end of last year. Overall, the backlog has been trending lower. But acquisitions and big contract wins are guaranteed to affect it in a positive light.

This is just one thing that investors should be paying attention to when management announces financial results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year before the market opens on July 29th. As the table below illustrates, analysts believe that revenue will contract from $188.7 million last year to $163 million this year. Earnings per share and overall net profits are expected to worsen, also. The table also shows cash flow figures for the second quarter of 2024. It will be interesting to see whether these worsen as well. Even so, as long as management doesn't materially alter guidance that has been put forward already, I don't see a reason to panic.

Takeaway

Although the last few years have been a difficult time for shareholders of Civeo Corporation, I believe that the company remains attractively priced. Recent moves made by management have increased the risk profile because of the rise in leverage that they necessitate. But at the same time, the major acquisition made by the firm reduces the overall trading multiple of the company and should boost financial results in the long run. Due to these factors, I have no problem keeping the company rated a ‘strong buy’ for now.

data.title: Fed Rate Decision In Focus With Microsoft, Apple Set To Report Earnings
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street was muted on Friday, with the major averages looking for clear direction as investors digested the previous session's record-setting finishes for the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite. From a sector perspective, eight of the S&P 500's 11 segments were in the green, led by strong performances in Consumer Discretionary and Industrials. On the flip side, Real Estate was lagging as the weakest performer of the day.

With a slate of earnings scheduled for next week, investors will also be occupied with major economic reports such as the Dallas Fed Manufacturing Business Index on Monday and Retail Inventories, Wholesale Inventories, and JOLTS Job Openings due on Tuesday. Wednesday will include important reports such as the MBA Purchase Index, Core PCE Prices, GDP Sales, and Fed interest rate decision. Thursday will see the release of Challenger Job Cuts and PCE Price Index, with Nonfarm payrolls and Michigan consumer sentiment to be released on Friday.

Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), Apple (AAPL), Amazon.com (AMZN), Mastercard (MA), Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), Visa (V), Procter & Gamble (PG), UnitedHealth (UNH), Boeing (BA), and PayPal (PYPL) will report earnings next week.

_____________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, July 28: Waste Management (WM), Nucor (NUE). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, July 29: Visa, Procter & Gamble, UnitedHealth, Boeing, PayPal. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, July 30: Microsoft, Meta Platforms, Qualcomm (QCOM), Robinhood (HOOD), eBay (EBAY). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, July 31: Apple, Amazon.com, Mastercard. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, August 1: Exxon Mobil, Chevron. See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Opendoor Technologies (OPEN) and Krispy Kreme (DNUT) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume and a surge in retail interest. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include NextSource (OTCQB:NSRCF), Aureus Greenway (AGH), and TE Connectivity (TEL). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Badger Meter (BMI), Molina (MOH), and Centene (CNC).

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include Constellation Brands (STZ), Morgan Stanley (MS), Texas Instruments (TXN), Delta Air Lines (DAL), A.O. Smith (AOS), Norfolk Southern (NSC), NRG Energy (NRG), and ONEOK (OKE). Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

IPO Watch: Ambiq Micro (AMBQ) is expected to price its IPO and begin to trade next week. The company develops ultra-low power semiconductor solutions for edge AI and general computing applications. Figma (FIG) is also expected to start trading. The design software company expects to sell about 37 million shares at $25 to $28 each, which would generate as much as $1 billion in proceeds. The IPO lockup period expires for certain blocks of shares of Smithfield Foods (SFD). The analyst quiet period expires on CoastalSouth Bancshares (COSO), CapsoVision (CV), Grande Group (GRAN), and Empro Group (EMPG) to free up analysts to post ratings.

Microsoft Earnings Preview: Microsoft is set to report its quarterly earnings next week, with analysts expecting earnings per share of $3.38 on a revenue of $73.8B. Microsoft's earnings are expected to be propelled by rapid AI adoption across its product ecosystem, continued strength in cloud services, and substantial investment in data center infrastructure. Looking ahead, investors will closely watch the pace of AI integration into core platforms like Azure, Office, and GitHub, as well as the company's ability to convert AI-driven demand into recurring revenue.

Apple Earnings Preview: Apple will report its earnings next week and is expected to report EPS of $1.43 on revenue of $88.89B. Analysts expect investor attention to center on the continued strength of subscription and service revenue. While concerns about slowing iPhone growth are valid and remain part of the bear case, the long-term investment story is increasingly shifting toward services.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Whitecap Resources: First Post-Merger Results & 2030 Projection Update
data.description:
In my previous Whitecap (TSX:WCP:CA) and (OTCPK:WCPRF) articles, I explained why I sold all my other oil producers and placed a large bet on the Veren-Whitecap merger. First, the dividend yield exceeded 9%, with room for further growth. Second, the downside was well protected.

The recent Q2 results show that everything is progressing as planned, with results strongly beating analysts' estimates. My projection model continues to show a double-digit yield, so I continue holding a 12% core portfolio position. That said, if the stock price were to rise another 20-30% with no improvement in oil/gas prices, I would consider trimming the position to ~8%.

Q2 Merger and Early Beat

Whitecap has officially merged with Veren as of May 12th, so the second quarter's production only reflects Veren's production from that date onward. This resulted in 292.8 kboe/d, with EPS of $0.33, beating estimates by $0.16.

What interests me more than short-term EPS results is the development of the announced C$200 million in annual savings from synergies. During the call, CFO Thanh Kang commented that he estimates C$35 million of synergies have already been achieved due to improvements in credit scoring and reductions in staff.

The merger is huge for the company as it is approximately doubling its scale. It's still too early for a detailed and reliable estimation, so we will have to wait for the numbers by early November, when the management plans to release the 2026 budget.

Since Veren's production numbers have been included since mid-May, Whitecap estimates achieving ~297,500 boe/d of production in 2025.

With a current production pace of 365,000 boe/d, $65 WTI, and C$3 AECO, management expects to generate around C$1.4 billion of free cash flow. The $900 million cost of the base dividend (6.8% yield) means management will have an additional $500 million to allocate between growth and buybacks.

What do I model going forward?

Despite having a sound macro-thesis about oil and gas, I believe that the commodity prices are nearly unpredictable; thus, I always think in scenarios.

Base Case

When I invest, my base case always considers the prices of commodity futures. In the end, the company can always hedge those prices, or so can you. It means that for the base case projection, I am using prices WTI $65, HH $4, and AECO C$3.

For the costs, royalties, and taxes, I am using guidance from page 25 of the presentation. Furthermore, I project C$35 million in synergy savings for 2025 and the full C$200 million in savings for 2026 onward.

We will have to wait for the November update about the growth plan, but for now, I model a C$500-C$700 million growth CapEx above the C$2 billion maintenance CapEx for 2026-2028. After that, I assume no growth capital, which results in an approximately 15% FCF yield that management can decide how to allocate.

For a closer look at the numbers, here is a graph of my projection for 2026 with those assumed commodity prices and production of 378 kboe/d.

Bull Case

Compared to the base case, I'm just adjusting the WTI to $80. Under such pricing, the company yield would quickly rise towards 20%, which would likely result in a doubling of the share price. The company would likely spend more on growth CapEx, as it has many options for growth post-merger.

The bull case could be further enhanced by higher-than-expected synergies. Management explained that the C$200 million is a base case, and they believe there will be more savings realized over time.

One more area that requires improvement and could potentially boost free cash flow is their natural gas marketing. For now, most of their gas is sold for AECO prices. The company targets 20% exposure to LNG pricing. There is no contract on the table at present, so it is not included in my projection. However, 20% of their gas linked to JKM pricing could generate an additional C$200-250 million in free cash flow. Additionally, a C$1 improvement in AECO yields an additional C$200 million in FCF.

Bear Case

My last article was pretty much all about the bear case. I took the company through the pricing from COVID lows, naked, without any hedging. The survival without the need for a dividend cut made me buy the large position. As of now, the company is hedging 22% of its oil production and 29% of its gas production for 2026, with debt down to C$3.3 billion, or 1x Net Debt/Funds Flow, due to non-core asset sales during the quarter.

Conclusion

Following the successful second quarter, the market now has greater confidence in the merger's execution. The share price has recovered sharply from the recent lows. That said, my model still yields a double-digit FCF under modest assumptions, so I am still holding Whitecap as my only oil holding.

If the price were to rise another 20–30% without an improvement in commodity prices, I would see the shares as fairly valued and would consider moving part of my oil allocation towards other oil names, such as Canadian Natural (CNQ), which has recently underperformed.

If you found this analysis useful, don’t forget to comment and follow for quarterly updates, deep scenario modeling, and risk-first positioning.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Venu Holding Keeps Adding Financial Complexity
data.description:
I wrote about Venu Holding (VENU) at the beginning of the year.

The company is developing amphitheaters, concert halls, and other entertainment venues via an innovative shared ownership methodology.

At the time, however, I considered that the complexity of the company's financials was too high to understand what the company's profitability and business looked like.

Now, two quarters (4Q24 and 1Q25) have passed, and complexity has skyrocketed. More layers of financial engineering have been added, with different claims on the underlying locations. During 2Q25, the company issued more debt and added preferred equity.

Underlying all this, the common stock has lost $17 million (almost 20% of equity) in one quarter, showing a risky trend.

Over that time, the company's common stock price has basically doubled, particularly since May 2025. I believe the name remains a Hold at least until the capital table is easier to understand, and that purchasing stock at these prices and stage of development is very risky.

Results And Operational Developments

Since I covered the name, Venu has released its financials for FY24 and 1Q25. The company also hosted an earnings call for FY24 results. The results show some interesting trends in the business.

Challenged revenues: 4Q24 and 1Q25 in conjunction show a worsening YoY trend of growth in the business (quarterly financials here), considering that Venu opened a large amphitheater in Colorado in August 2024. Revenues went up 34% in 3Q24, but only 9% in 4Q24 and then decreased 11% in 1Q25.

This might be affected by season, as it is unlikely that there is a lot of entertainment activity during winter in an open amphitheater in Colorado, but it is still a trend to follow.

Worsening gross margins: Potentially because of the revenue decrease, and a degree of operational leverage in CoGS (labor, rent and D&A are probably not as variable as food expenses), the company's gross profits also decreased, from $1.4 million in 1Q24 and $2.9 million in 3Q24, to $0.9 million in 1Q25.

Higher OpEx, larger sequential losses: Despite moderating growth and shrinking gross profits, operational expenses continued to grow, leading to larger operational losses. OpEx went from $6.6 million in 3Q24 to $7.9 million in 4Q24 and $19.5 million in 1Q25. In 1Q25, we might remove $11.5 million in stock compensation (more on this later) as non-recurring, but the figure still shows SG&A growth, at $8 million.

No operational profits in the venues: This is the operational portion of the income statement, where we are talking about the entertainment venues before the financing complexity of those venues. So far, the company cannot generate a profit from them. If anything, the existing venues generate a loss of $7 million per quarter (without considering SBC). During the FY24 earnings call, management considered that after more venues are opened, FY26 should be the year when the company is operationally profitable.

Moderate operational advances: Compared to the impressive financial activity that we will review below, the company's operational advances are more moderate. The company announced that it had closed on the property for the McKinney, TX stadium in January 2025. Construction should start in Spring 2025. The company also announced that it had changed the design of the McKinney amphitheater to allow a year-round configuration that would allow part of the stadium to be used during the cold months.

Financial Complexity Balloons

In my original article on the name, one of the main points in my Hold rating was that Venu's financial complexity was too high. At the time, the complexity stemmed from two points.

First, the sale of non-controlling interests with unclear rights on the stadiums, via the sale of 'fire-pits' and 'seats'. On the financial statements, these show as non-controlling interests (a form of equity), but on the promotion of these sales, the company uses the wording 'guaranteed rents', which is much more confusing (the wording guaranteed rents is used in many of the company's press releases, like here, here, and here). The word guaranteed might imply that those non-controlling interests might have a priority over the common in earnings.

Second, the company had an operational agreement for its largest venue (with the firm AEG), which implied the sharing of profits from the stadium on a 50/50% basis, without clarifying if that was also accounted as a non-controlling interest or not, and if the profits were distributed before or after the remaining non-controlling interests.

To this, we now add many additional forms of complexity that make understanding the company's future financing and profitability a rather daunting task, in my opinion.

Land debt: The company added $25 million in non-interest debt from the City of McKinney, as part of its purchase of the land for the new amphitheater. This is very positive financing, as it pays no interest. Two of the company's shareholders (one being its CEO) provided guarantees to the company, and received $7.5 million in equity compensation for that service. I would believe that the level of compensation for a guarantee is excessive, compared to the compensation for the guarantee given the size of the loan.

The company also reported receiving $8 million from the city of El Paso in Texas as another interest-free loan to develop a venue there, with the potential to get the loan forgiven if the construction is completed before a maturity date.

Convertible debt: The company issued $6 million in additional convertible debt in 1Q25, and according to the 10-Q, it issued $12 million more after the quarter ended, which added to the previous $9 million result in a total of $27 million in convertible debts, at different strikes, but with an interest rate of 12%. This already represents a $3.24 million interest expense per year, payable in cash or shares at the election of the lender.

The 10-Q clarifies that 'The principal is secured by certain real property of the Company', but without mentioning which property. Further, is that debt sitting after the non-controlling interests or before them?

Potential sale-leaseback: During the earnings call, management said that it is planning to sell the land on which the Colorado Springs stadium is situated and lease it back. This would generate an (accounting) gain from the sale, some cash financing, and add another line of recurring costs (leases are akin to debt). I would not know if the lease priority sits before or after the non-controlling interests.

Preferred shares: After the end of the quarter, the company issued 8% preferred debt, for an undisclosed amount (the SEC registration allows for up to $71 million). The preferred shares will be callable at par ($15 per share) in 2030. Usually, preferred debt sits just above common. I would speculate that in this case, it sits after non-controlling interests, but I have no way to know. We will need to wait for the specific data on how much was issued to understand the cost (from the perspective of the common, which we are analyzing here).

Financed sales of seats: I thought the whole point of selling the seats that become non-controlling interests in the company was to finance the construction of the entertainment venues.

However, the company has now introduced a 25% down, 75% amortized over 20 years methodology for purchasing the seats (10-Q under 'structured financing model'). This is not financed by a third-party (so far), but rather by the company.

The accounting is even more complex than regular seats. First, the company records a deposit (in cash) and then a receivable, which is a contra to the non-controlling interest. So, non-controlling interests grow, but they are in part offset by the receivables.

Still, the company said that it had sold $38 million in seats in 1Q25 (press release), and non-controlling interest went up $35 million in the 10-Q, but the cash received from the sale of non-controlling interests was only $16 million (Cash Flow Statement), and the company reported on the 10-Q that it had only sold $13 million in financed seats, meaning cash from non-controlling interests should have been $28 million ($25 million from non-financed, plus $3 million from the deposits).

There is no information on the amortization schedule, interest rates to be charged, or whether the non-controlling interests already accrue their (also undisclosed) rights.

Income fund launched: The company also announced the launch of an Income Fund that will raise as much as $59 million to buy non-controlling interests. This would not add much complexity, considering that the fund is just a large owner of this non-controlling interest, but the fund is being raised by one of the company's subsidiaries (Venu Income LLC, listed in the 10-Q).

Triple net lease: The company is now also offering triple net lease as an additional structure, which is marketed under the same name as the non-controlled interests (FireSuite), but I would not know if it has the same rights as a regular FireSuite. Again, same questions, where does it sit in the complex operational, debt, and equity cap table that is Venu?

Worrying common equity trend: As all of this complexity adds up, one of the lines in the company's common equity continues to fall, at a worrying rate. The company lost $17 million in common equity in 1Q25 (from $95 million in 4Q24, or almost 20%).

Cash situation is hard to predict: As of 1Q25, the company had $25 million in cash and short-term investments, falling by $13 million QoQ.

The company's operational cash flows were pretty bad, at a negative $9 million (coincident with the operational loss after adding back SBC). In addition, the company invested $24 million and received funds from its different financing mechanisms for $20 million net.

After the quarter, the company has not announced sales of non-controlling interests, as it did before 1Q25 every month. However, we can add $12 million in convertible debt, an undisclosed amount of funds from the preferred debt, and the potential funds from the Income Fund, the net lease sales, and the regular FireSuite sales.

I would not believe that the company will face financing constraints soon, because of the amount of funds received. However, the company has already sold part of, and committed to building, several entertainment venues. At least three have started sales without finalizing construction: McKinney, TX, Broken Arrow, OK, and Mustang Creek, OK. That means the company has to invest probably quite a sum in CAPEX. According to some sources, the Colorado Springs amphitheater cost $110 million.

Venu Is Too Complex And Expensive For Investment

In conclusion, I believe the name's financing structure is so complex and in constant flux that it is very hard for the common shareholder to understand the potential value of the claims to which the share entitles him/her.

This is the basis for my opinion about Venu as an investment. It is hard to understand how much profitability is required before the common equity sees any money.

This would already be hard for a company embarking on several construction projects at the same time, with operational losses on the projects it already owns, and with massive CAPEX commitments, even if the financing was very simple (say a single chunk of debt or preferred equity).

But in addition to this, even if Venu was only a speculative bet on the company's management or in the projects, the stock is very expensive. Venu trades at a market cap of $550 million, which is 7x its book value (to common), and 30x its TTM sales. There is no multiple on any form of earnings (EBITDA, EBIT, Net Income) as there are none.

For these reasons, I believe Venu is not an interesting investment, and maintain a Hold rating. I prefer to wait until there is more clarity around the capital structure, financing, and economics of the venues.

data.title: A 13% Yield Coiled Spring Ready To Pop: BrightSpire Capital
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Farmers have a complex task of deciding what to grow and identifying the niche they want to enter. If you look up the 15 most successful and profitable crops for small farms, the vast majority of them have to be planted every single year and grow to full size within 30 to 90 days. This means that much of the year, your farm will either need to be replanted and growing again if you live somewhere in the south where that is possible, or your farm may be idle and need an alternative source of income.

You can get on average between $1.50 and $5 per pound of lettuce. Perhaps, though, if you want to be a farmer who wants to think a little longer term, you could plant an orchard of cherry trees, which gather $1.50-$5 per pound, and the average tree can produce 100-150 pounds of cherries per year. This would be something where you plant it once and continue to harvest the produce from it over decades. You first, though, have to have the capital and the sustainability to afford waiting for your trees to reach fruiting age and maximum production.

When it comes to building a portfolio in the stock market, I'm a great believer in investing in immediate income investments. This means that my investments are paying me a high yield now. This enables me to invest in opportunities that are higher yielding but may not bear full fruit and benefit for a couple of years out. Unlike many traders who buy something and plan to sell within 6 to 12 months or even less, I buy and hold for years. This means that I'm willing to buy something that is in the germination stage of development and allow it to grow in my Portfolio, carefully watched over.

Today, I want to look at an opportunity that carries a great yield now, but the real benefits of holding it won't come to fruition for potentially a couple of years from now.

Let's dive in!

Pushing Down On A Coiled Spring

BrightSpire Capital, Inc. (NYSE:BRSP), yielding 13%, is a commercial mortgage REIT. In theory, commercial mortgage REITs thrive in high-interest-rate environments because they are invested in variable debt. However, a combination of rapidly rising rates and weakening real estate fundamentals led to numerous defaults. Many commercial mortgage REITs were unable to leverage up and take advantage of higher rates, needing to be more cautious to manage their non-accruals.

In the earnings call, CEO Mike Mazzei was straightforward with what will be needed to get the dividend to $0.20/quarter:

"Let me make this a lot easier. What we need to do to get back to circa $0.20 a share is we need to get the portfolio back up to $3.5 billion. And right now, it stands at $2.4 billion. So, we need to keep abreast of payments, and we have to have a net gain on the portfolio between now and, let's say, a year from now of about $1 billion net gain to about $3.5 billion, which would bring us up to about low 3 times leverage, 3.3 times leverage on the portfolio if we get there."

There are two aspects to the size of the portfolio. First, there is the amount that BRSP is capable of originating. Originating a mortgage requires finding someone who wants a mortgage, underwriting the building, negotiating the loan, and then the deal actually closing and being funded. Sometimes, a lender might work to originate a loan for an acquisition, but then the real estate sale never happens. The lender typically doesn't have a lot of say on when a closing happens and has no power to accelerate it. So, BRSP's ability to originate mortgages is determined by the opportunities presented, those opportunities being acceptable to BRSP and the underlying real estate transactions closing. BRSP can control its own activity in terms of putting itself out there as a lender open to business, but the level of others' activity is out of its control.

The second factor is the loans that are being paid off or are in default. Source

In Q1, BRSP funded $116 million in loans, but there were $133 million in repayments, and $33 million was a loan that was foreclosed on, and the value was moved to REO (Real Estate Owned). As a result, the overall size of the loan portfolio shrank.

In Q2, we expect that BRSP's portfolio will shrink more as it resolved its largest non-performing loan. The San Jose hotel was foreclosed on and transferred into BRSP's possession in June. That hotel was carried at a value of $135 million, and will be moved into REO with a new value of $80 million – the price that BRSP bid at the foreclosure auction.

BRSP had two properties on non-accrual, with the San Jose loan being the largest one:

As a result of that transaction, BRSP will see a decline in book value because it will record a $55 million "loss", and its loan portfolio will shrink. However, in terms of cash flow, BRSP will start collecting the net income produced by the hotel. It was collecting nothing, now it will be collecting something. At some point in the future, BRSP will sell the Signia Hotel, and it will realize a gain or loss upon the $80 million value. At the Q2 earnings call, we expect management to discuss how it plans to maximize the value of the property to sell it for a higher price.

So, for Q2, we don't expect BRSP to make substantial progress in growing its portfolio on paper. We expect that book value will decline due to the write-off caused by the hotel being moved from a loan to being owned real estate. We expect that cash flow will be relatively unchanged in Q2.

On the positive side, BRSP's non-accrual rate is going to be dramatically lower, and its watchlist will account for a much smaller portion of its portfolio. Knowing what is happening with the Signia Hotel and knowing that BRSP will start seeing cash flow from that property in Q3 is a positive. The amount of uncertainty surrounding BRSP is now much lower, and that is typically a good thing for the market.

The bottom line is that we don't think BRSP is going to build up an extra $1 billion this year. We expect that the dividend will remain at $0.16/quarter. We further expect that we will have to wait until Q3 to start seeing some positive growth in the loan portfolio. BRSP has spent most of the past year in the $5-$6 range, and we expect that to continue for the next few months. I'm very happy to be buying at these prices, getting a healthy yield today with an eye on returning to that $0.20/quarter dividend probably in late 2026 or early 2027

Conclusion

At current prices, BRSP offers an attractive 13% yield for those who are buying shares at these prices. The dividend remains a question of what will occur in the future, but the company's CEO has been extremely open. The dividend isn't increasing until the portfolio size is large enough to support it. This makes sense since BRSP is an REIT and is required to distribute most of its taxable earnings as dividends to its shareholders. There is no need to pay an oversized dividend that they can't afford, only to dwindle the ability to pay it in the future. Likewise, the company needs to expand its portfolio to sustain dividend growth. Currently, BRSP is in the trenches, along with many other companies exposed to commercial real estate, as they seek the best path forward for their holdings. We highlighted previously how another wonderful commercial real estate REIT is switching to an aggressive offensive footing after being careful navigating the current environment, and BRSP is starting to position itself that way as well.

Your retirement is going to hopefully be about 30 years, if not longer. This means that you shouldn't invest only with a 1-2 year mindset, but rather with a mindset that considers decades of future needs and benefits to enjoy. I collect income today to reinvest it and to enjoy it today with an eye on the future. My portfolio will continue to provide me with a wonderful income over those 30 years, and I can also continue to grow that income over time. This is why my portfolio of income investments found within High Dividend Opportunities is designed to provide income growth as well as strong portfolio guidance rules that ensure a high degree of diversification and a reduction of risk where possible. Your portfolio should be designed to provide for you today and tomorrow. Unfortunately, too many investors focus on just tomorrow and forget today, or go all in on today and forget tomorrow. The balance for you is going to be what can make you comfortable in both situations, and building a portfolio to meet that.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Solesence Is Growing A Lot, But The Stock Price Is Several Years In The Future
data.description:
Solesence (SLSN) is a cosmetics CDMO that manufactures for skin-care third-party brands. The company's consumer-product segment (called Solesence) has been incredibly successful, multiplying revenues by 20x in the past four years.

On the margin side, the company has not been super able to leverage production margins, but has been able to control SG&A very positively, translating that revenue growth into some profitability growth.

The company also ticks other quality boxes, like low leverage and a strong controlling shareholder.

On the other hand, the company's valuation is certainly not low. The name trades at 5x sales or more, and even assuming significant growth going forward, it would be years before it could print even a moderate earnings yield. I think the market is a little ahead of developments, and therefore initiate the name with a Hold despite its very interesting characteristics.

From Ingredients To Finished Products

For many years, Solesence traded under the name Nanophase Technologies, with the ticker NANX.

The company's business had been the manufacturing of certain specialty chemicals that are used in cosmetics. For years, the company's main client had been BASF, under which the company manufactured zinc and titanium oxide products (see Zinc Oxide Supply Agreement on the latest 10-K). In 2019, BASF represented 65% of the company's revenues.

Then, in 2016, the company launched a new segment called Solesence, which also offered CDMO services, but instead of offering ingredients, manufactured finished products.

By 2020, Solesence (the segment) had grown from zero to about $2 million in revenues, and from then on, its revenues exploded. Today, Solesence (segment) represents 80% of the company's revenues, and the company adopted the name of the segment.

End Demand Is Hard To Evaluate

The company has mentioned some client names, like Colorescience, Tatcha, and Credo (all from the 1Q25 earnings call).

We also know from the company's 10-K for FY24 that concentration is relatively high. One customer represented 32% of sales, and another one 7% of sales (meaning between these two, about half of the finished product segment sales).

When I search for the brand relevance of these customers on Google Trends, they do not show such explosive growth, not even close. It might be that Solesence has gained a consistently larger share of these customers, manufacturing, and that therefore growth comes not as much from end-demand as from share of pocket.

Margin Leverage With Challenges

In terms of margins, Solesence has shown positive dynamics.

On the one hand, gross margins have been pretty flat, despite a tripling of revenues in the last four years and a shift to a more downstream segment. I do not think this is problematic, but rather the contrary.

Ramping production is hard, and companies may find challenges in production. For example, in FY22, the company's gross and operating margins fell significantly (moving to operational losses, see below) because of operational challenges with ramp-up. In 1Q25, something similar happened, as commented on their call. Judging by the comments from analysts and shareholders on the 1Q25 call, the operational challenges have been a constant problem. I do not think this is strange for a company growing the revenues of a segment by 20x in a few years.

Once Solesence's revenues stabilize, I believe their gross margins can grow via efficiencies.

Even more positive, Solesence's OpEx has remained very well controlled. Indeed, OpEx has grown, but at a compounded rate that is 1/3 of revenues. Further, in the past two years, OpEx has been fairly flat while revenues doubled again.

Controlled OpEx is the basis of profitability in a volume business like CDMO, because it allows the business to post operational leverage. Unsurprisingly, Solesence has moved from consistent negative profitability to breakeven and then to profits with margins of almost 10%.

Committed Management And Ownership

The company's CEO joined the company in 2009. As the company was small back then, he also filled the roles of CFO, to this day. Salary and compensation are relatively moderate. For example, in 2023 and 2024, each CEO and COO received about $400 thousand each, with very little in the form of bonuses. In many companies, growing revenues 100% twice, managers tend to receive large stock prizes (see the latest proxy).

This control over expenses and over management might come from the company's controlling shareholder. One of the company's shareholders owned 72% of the company in 2024, and his wife is the Chairman of the Board. The Board has three members only (one is the CEO), and made less than $100 thousand in total compensation in 2024.

I generally like companies with managements and Boards that are paid correctly but not excessively, and with controlling shareholders that have a lot of skin in the game.

External Financing Might Be Needed

As of 1Q25, Solesence cash balances were $1.8 million, added total current assets of about $34 million, against current liabilities of $33 million, and no long-term debt.

The company has access to two lines of credit, but the exact amount is not clear. From the 10-K liquidity section, I calculate about $13 million, which means about $6 million is already in use.

It should be noted that the companies providing the credit lines to Solesence are related to the company's largest shareholder (see Item 10 of the 10-K).

Further, last year alone, the company required an equity injection, provided by one of the line of credit providers. Strandler (one of the lenders) bought 15,000 preferred shares at $400 each ($6 million in financing). The shares were converted into 15 million common shares in late 2024. At the time of the purchase of the preferred, the company's stock price was about $0.5 to $0.6 per common, meaning each preferred had a conversion value of $500 to $600, and was therefore issued at a discount. This is normal considering that the deal was large.

The company has been operationally profitable for four out of the past five quarters, but it has significant capital requirements. As will be seen below, the company needs to grow equity at about the same rate as it grows revenues. For example, if it grows at 25% per year (currently growing at 50% per year), its equity needs to grow by that same rate (in this case, about $4 million).

The company currently cannot finance that from operations alone (TTM net income is less than $3.5 million, and the company is growing at 50%). The company can use the working capital facilities, but without additional expansions or additions to those facilities, it may need to issue equity again, or slow down its growth.

This dynamic should be monitored over time.

The valuation is hard to justify, even with tremendous optimism

If I have spoken so positively of Solesence, then why is my rating a Hold? Because of valuation, which is the balance of the company's quality in any stock.

Just as it happens with many high-growth names, Solesence's stock price reflects not only its current quality characteristics, but also a lot of what will happen in the future. The problem with such an approach is that performing well becomes a necessity, and not an opportunity. If the company does not meet a very high bar, then the stock price might crater, even if the company's operations are improving.

To understand how big this optimism is in Solesence's price, a simple profitability model will suffice. The company has a market cap of $330 million. Let us calculate how much growth would be needed to obtain an FCF yield of 5% on the name, which, added to a 10% growth yield, would represent a relatively attractive 15% total return yield. This is, assuming fixed earnings multiples, and with a subjective determination of an attractive yield, based on what I believe a high growth but also still small and nascent name should return. The attractive yield number is much less important than the calculations showing how much optimism is needed to reach that yield.

Let us consider that the company can grow gross margins to 30%, from the current ~25%, and above what has been its historical average. Further, let us consider that from the current 20%, OpEx stabilizes around 15%. OpEx leverage cannot be maintained forever in almost no company, because overhead is always required. Still, this would imply a company with an operating margin of 15%, which can be considered a high margin.

In terms of capital, despite tremendous growth in revenues, Solesence's asset utilization has been flattish, and it has not been able to finance that growth via working capital (otherwise, assets to equity would have grown), meaning the company needs to invest at least the same amount as it grows. For example, if the company grows 10%, its equity will need to grow 10% to sustain working capital and PP&E needs.

Solesence has about $12 million in debt and pays around 7% on it, for a total cost of about $700 thousand per year. Assuming the company maintains that level of financing going forward (credit facilities approximately in size with equity or 1/3 of revenues at constant asset utilization, paying 7%), it should devote about 2% of revenues to interest payments.

After interest and taxes of 25%, the company's net margin would be about 10%. With equity representing approximately 1/3 of revenues, the growth of 10% requires approximately 3% of revenues every year. That means the after-tax, after capital investment margin (an FCF margin) would be about 7% of revenues.

Of course, these are speculative figures, but I believe they make sense as an estimation of the company's potential economics. Above all, I believe they are rather optimistic, more than conservative, as they assume operating margins of about 15%.

Now, to generate a 5% yield on its current market cap of $330 million, Solesence would need to generate an FCF of $16.5 million. Based on my calculations above, that figure can be achieved with revenues of $235 million (based on an FCF margin after growth of 8%).

That means the company's stock price already incorporates more than 4x the revenues the company generates today. Even growing at 50% per year, the company's current YoY growth rate, it would take more than three years to grow that much.

That is, Solesence is in some way already pricing more than 300% revenue growth, double its operating margins, and 10% growth going forward. If some of those do not become a reality, then the stock price might suffer. Further, if these are met, only then is the valuation attractive.

For that reason, in my opinion, Solesence is not attractive at these prices. The company is performing very well, but the stock price has gone too far into the future.

data.title: Golub Capital: This Is Why We Are Likely To Talk About Dividend Cut Soon
data.description:
As we entered 2025, I issued an article comparing two rather popular BDCs, which operate mostly in the traditional first-lien space: Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) and Golub Capital BDC (GBDC).

The idea of the article was to compare them both side by side in order to determine, which has superior risk-adjusted return potential going forward.

Even though the GBDC had much more favorable valuation, I still opted to go long BXSL. There were multiple drivers behind that, but the key one was the lack of sufficient margin of safety on GBDC's base dividend coverage front.

Here in the chart below, we can see that since the publication of my piece, GBDC has slightly underperformed both BXSL and the broader BDC market (BIZD).

If we strip out the collected dividends, GBDC is actually down by almost 2%, which, theoretically, could suggest that the entry point has become a bit more attractive for investors. Plus, the difference between BXSL's P/NAV and that of GBDC has widened to roughly 15%. Currently, GBDC's P/NAV is 1.03x, which implies that we are not paying too much of a premium (although, compared to sector median, we are paying 8% more).

Another noteworthy aspect is the combination of the current dividend yield of 11.4% (a very attractive level) and the fact that GBDC has managed to accommodate rather stable dividend streams all the way starting in 2010, when it tapped the IPO market. Here is a visual representation of that.

So, at least from the surface, GBDC's investment case looks quite enticing - i.e., an acceptable P/NAV and ~11.4% yield with, theoretically (based on history), the necessary income durability in place.

Let me, however, highlight several points why I am actually not that bullish on this case.

Thesis Review

First of all, we have to understand that by buying GBDC, we are agreeing to pay exactly the price that is associated with the underlying NAV. In fact, we are paying a slight premium, especially compared to the sector median.

Of course, there are such players as Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN), Hercules Capital (HTGC) and Capital Southwest (CSWC) that carry more than 35% premiums to their reported NAVs, which suggests that paying extra is not necessarily a reason to not invest. Namely, provided that the right fundamentals are there, it makes sense to pay more.

However, most of these BDCs, which trade above NAV, are associated with internal management schemes, high equity investment exposures, very conservative debt loads, or distinct advantages in the origination front - think of Ares Capital (ARCC).

GBDC has none of these characteristics.

Granted, this is why we are not paying a notable premium to NAV, but then again - I would like to actually see some form of a discount for GBDC.

Here is why.

Since the publication of my prior article on GBDC, the NAV per share value has been consistently declining. In all of the four previously reported financial quarters, GBDC has registered a NAV decay.

The reason is simple - it is a combination of non-performing loan write-downs and declining spreads. The former is a BDC-specific issue and the latter is something that is of a systematic nature, where we should not blame the management.

So, if the NAV per share continues to slide down quarter by quarter, then by buying GBDC now at a slight premium, we are actually assuming a larger (forward-looking) premium, which again questions the overall case here.

Now, it might not be an issue provided that GBDC can halt the NAV-erosion process and, more importantly, keep the current dividends intact in a sustainable and long-term fashion.

And this is where I become skeptical.

First, currently, GBDC has its dividend just barely covered by the NII per share generation. The base dividend coverage ratio stands at 100%, which a) does not leave any margin of safety in place and b) is more aggressive than the sector average of 103% (and remember, the sector average is comprised of many underperforming/speculative cases).

Second, I do not see how GBDC could get out of this situation unless there is a notable change in the systematic drivers, which is clearly out of GBDC's control (rising spreads, higher base rates, sharply recovering M&A markets). The biggest headwind is the forthcoming pressures associated with declining spreads. As of the most recent financial report, GBDC has a weighted average portfolio investment yield of 10.8%, while the new loans that are coming in and gradually replacing the old ones are yielding below 10% (9.4% and 9.7% for Q1 and Q2, 2025, respectively).

Third, there are actually two ways to work yourself out of this kind of a situation. The first one is through a considerable portfolio growth, where the "volume" aspect could offset the "price" aspect of the business. Here I have to mention my favorite BDC - Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC) - which has one of the least leveraged balance sheets in the sector, allowing it to employ a "volume-focused" strategy. The second is monetizing high-quality equity stakes in portfolio investment companies. This is where the previously mentioned BDCs - MAIN and CSWC - thrive. However, in GBDC's case we see a debt-saturated balance sheet that has a higher debt load than for the sector average peer. Plus, GBDC has not strong enough equity investment exposure to rely on this strategy (plus, a large part of the equity investments has stemmed from first-lien non-accrual restructuring).

Finally, GBDC has two considerable unsecured notes with clearly below market-level yields maturing in 2026 and 2027 - 2.5% and 2.0%, respectively. Together they both comprise ~19% of the total leverage, which is something that will definitely move the needle one through refinancings the yields would come closer to the 5.5% - 6.5%, which is where the prevailing investment grade BDC cost of debt levels are.

The Bottom Line

If I put all of these pieces together, I clearly struggle to see a justified investment case. It seems that GBDC's earnings are set to go down at least in the short to medium-term. And I have not even factored in the consequences from potentially decreasing base rates and incremental non-accruals, which would not be that surprising given the overall uncertainty in the system.

In my humble opinion, we will see a further NII per share reduction in the upcoming Q3, 2025 earnings report, driven by a continued spread compression and negative net investment volumes in Q2 (that lowered the portfolio base). This would then send the base dividend coverage level below 100%, which should give a substantiated ground for the market to initiate some form of repricing to the downside.

Having said that, given GBDC's dividend distribution track record, I do not see a dividend cut risk now. The BDC has a meaningful amount of undistributed spillover income that it could use to avoid the cut. However, this is inherently a temporary (debt-driven) solution. In my view, we will be talking about a potential dividend cut if and/or when the FED cuts interest rates by at least 50 basis points - i.e., it would drive down the NII into an even more unsustainable territory.

So far, GBDC is not a buy for me.

data.title: Crypto ETFs: Regulation, Returns And Rise Of Innovation
data.description:
The past week was a significant moment for the crypto landscape, both in terms of legislation and market momentum. While not all the regulatory developments directly impact crypto ETFs, the effect of increased regulatory clarity and institutional adoption benefits the space broadly. Here’s a closer look at the evolving regulatory backdrop, fund flows, and new product developments across the crypto ETF ecosystem.

More regulatory support…

Last week was dubbed “Crypto Week.” Three major bills made headlines: the GENIUS Act, the Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act, and the Clarity Act. Each of these represents a step toward building a more defined framework for digital assets. That is something that has been a persistent hurdle for the industry. Several of these bills are loosely related to crypto ETFs. Regardless, regulatory progress is contributing to overall optimistic sentiment in this space.

  • The GENIUS Act (Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for U.S. Stablecoins) addresses the issuance and oversight of stablecoins. This act has officially been signed into law. Ethereum (ETH-USD) plays a major role in stablecoin operations and has reacted positively to this news.
  • The Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act prevents the Federal Reserve from issuing a central bank digital currency (CBDC) directly to consumers due to surveillance and privacy concerns.
  • The CLARITY Act is particularly relevant to the ETF space, since it addresses the ongoing ambiguity over whether certain tokens fall under the jurisdiction of the SEC or CFTC — essentially the longstanding “security vs. commodity” debate.
  • Additionally, the SEC made a statement in early July addressing some of the disclosure requirements for crypto asset exchange traded products.

The CLARITY Act and the Anti-CBDC Act still need Senate approval. But overall, these regulations have already contributed to improving investor sentiment. I expect to see an influx of approvals later this year — including spot ETFs, multi-token ETFs (discussed in more detail below), staking, and in-kind redemptions — after a period of internal alignment.

…leads to stronger institutional adoption…

Optimism around regulatory support discussed above has led to renewed enthusiasm in the cryptomarkets, particularly stronger institutional interest.

Bitcoin recently crossed $120,000, while spot bitcoin ETFs saw approximately $8 billion in net inflows over the past month. That brings the year-to-date total to over $19 billion. Most of this activity has been concentrated in the iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT) which saw around $6 billion in net inflows this past month.

Similarly, ether has rebounded sharply, rising around 60% in the past month. While ether ETFs have seen a total of just under $6 billion in net inflows this year, over half of that — $3 billion — came in just the last month after a previously slow year.

…and product innovation including altcoin ETFs…

Along with more institutional adoption, investor interest is widening to include altcoins and multi-asset crypto exposure.

The REX-Osprey SOL + Staking ETF (SSK), which was launched earlier this month, is both the first spot Solana (SOL-USD) and the first to offer staking. The nine other proposed spot Solana ETFs were filed by the usual players like Fidelity, VanEck, and 21Shares under the ’33 Act — the same structure as both spot bitcoin and ether ETFs. SSK, however, is a ’40 Act fund. This requires it to invest through a diversified portfolio of other securities. Currently, it includes the 21Shares Solana ETP, at around 40% of the fund’s weight. While this means it’s not a “traditional spot product,” SSK gained early traction due to its first-mover advantage, with assets growing to around $100 million within less than a month.

In addition to SSK, leveraged and derivatives-based Solana and XRP products have entered the market, including products from ProShares, Volatility Shares, and Teucrium. While these haven’t taken off significantly, they are still gathering a fair amount of attention, especially given the already crowded market. The Teucrium 2x Long Daily XRP ETF (XXRP) recently saw over $160 million in net inflows over the past month.

…and multi-token ETFs.

The next step for crypto ETFs will likely be multitoken products. Grayscale’s Digital Large Cap Fund (OTC:GDLC), which includes approximately 75% bitcoin, 15% ether, and smaller allocations to Solana, XRP, and Cardano, received initial approval but was placed on hold by the SEC. A similar approval-then-hold process happened to Bitwise’s application to convert its Bitwise 10 Crypto Index Fund (OTC:BITW) into an ETF. Like GDLC, BITW also holds a large weight in bitcoin and ether (90%) with the remaining 10% in Solana, XRP, Cardano, Avalanche, Chainlink, Bitcoin Cash, Uniswap, and Polkadot.

These pauses aren’t denials. They more likely reflect the need to align with updated SEC regulations, including the guidance issued in early July, which outlined disclosure standards for crypto products. The uniqueness of these multitoken funds stems from their structure since they include tokens that don’t yet have spot ETFs (XRP, Cardano, etc.). That creates two possibilities: 1) SEC approval of multitoken funds could signal green lights for several of these spot ETFs; or 2) alternatively, the small allocations may allow for approval without broader spot fund precedents.

Either way, GDLC and BITW present a test for how the SEC will treat diversified crypto exposure under the evolving framework. A filing for a similar product (Crypto Blue Chip ETF) tied to Trump Media in partnership with Yorkville and Crypto.com also recently made headlines. This product looks similar to GDLC but substitutes Cardano for Cronos (CRO).

Bottom Line: As more regulatory clarity emerges, I expect to see increasing product innovation and broader institutional adoption. Both of those could further solidify crypto’s role in the ETF ecosystem and traditional finance.

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2025. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit VettaFi.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Paramount: The FCC Just Unlocked The Next Chapter
data.description:
Paramount Global (PARA) (NASDAQ:PARAA) just got the green light that changes everything. After nearly nine months of limbo, the FCC has formally approved the Skydance-Paramount merger. The delay is over. The Redstone era is over. And the next chapter for this iconic media company is officially underway.

This is a follow-up to my original buy recommendation on the stock about a year ago. The deal injects billions in fresh capital and removes the legacy baggage that’s weighed on the stock for years. With the right execution, I believe PARA is still significantly undervalued, and the path to a $25-$30 stock is now clearer than ever.

Skydance

David Ellison and RedBird Capital didn’t just acquire a company, they acquired one of the most undervalued media platforms in the public markets. Paramount owns CBS, Pluto TV, a deep studio back catalog, and a globally recognized IP like “Top Gun,” “Mission: Impossible,” and “SpongeBob.” All of it, until now, has been trapped under misaligned leadership, bloated cost structures, and a highly levered balance sheet. That’s what’s changing.

The deal terms remain intact: $2.4B to buy out National Amusements and eliminate Shari Redstone’s control; $1.5B in capital injected directly into PARA’s balance sheet; and $4.5B in value returned to shareholders via $15/share stock or cash election. Yes, there’s dilution: 317M new Class B shares and another 200M warrants at a $30.50 strike. But that’s not a downside. That’s actually good for a stock that could double from today’s levels.

The structure is aggressive by design. Ellison and Shell want to win, and they only win big if shareholders win big.

New Management

For the first time in years, PARA has operators at the top, not legacy caretakers. David Ellison isn’t just the son of a billionaire, he’s run a successful studio with a proven track record of commercial hits. He knows the content business. Also, Jeff Shell, Chair of Redbird Capital and former CEO of NBCUniversal, brings the scale discipline and turnaround experience that this company desperately needed.

I think they are here to rebuild.

Paramount+ is going under the knife. The current streaming model is unprofitable. The new vision is to create a differentiated platform focused on retention and monetization.

Meanwhile, Pluto TV is finally getting its due. This is an undermonetized asset with massive user reach, growing ad inventory, and untapped margin potential. Ellison wants to double down, and rightly so. At scale, this could be a multi-billion dollar revenue stream with minimal incremental investment.

Shell and Ellison aren’t afraid to trim the fat. $2B in cost synergies are on deck. Redundant teams across CBS, Comedy Central, and MTV could be consolidated or sold. Non-core broadcast stations are likely headed to auction. These are the cost cutting measures investors have wanted for years, and it’s finally happening.

The Balance Sheet

Paramount’s Achilles heel has always been its debt. The $1.5B equity raise, plus the $2B in savings, materially shifts the company’s financial profile. Net leverage is projected to fall below 2.5x by 2027.

Importantly, there are no major maturities until 2030. That gives Ellison and team ample time to execute the turnaround without being forced into distressed asset sales or refinancing in this interest rate environment. With the balance sheet stabilized, every dollar can now be focused on driving content bets, monetization of assets like Pluto, or even acquisitions, if the right price allows it.

This new capital structure is built to win, and it’s exactly what the old regime never had the will or ability to pursue.

The Stock

Despite all the changes, PARA still trades like a distressed company. It’s not. Book value remains well north of $25/share.

The $15 headline price in the Skydance deal wasn’t a premium, it was more of an entry point. The real upside comes from what’s next.

Wall Street consensus sees EPS $1.21 in 2025, but that’s before cost synergies and Pluto upside are fully reflected. If margins expand even modestly and the platform approach starts working, this business could be earning closer to $2.00/share by 2026.

At a 11-14x multiple, you’re looking at a $22-$28 stock - with upside to $35 if Ellison’s vision plays out. And with 200M warrants sitting at a $30.50 strike, there’s every incentive for the new management team to get the stock there.

Risks

First, the dilution isn’t small. Between the new Class B shares and the warrants, this becomes a much bigger share count. Yes, it’s strategic. Yes, it funds the turnaround. But it also means the road to higher EPS could potentially take some time.

Second, the streaming pivot is risky. Paramount+ has been bleeding money, and while Ellison’s new approach sounds smart -- leaner, more targeted, and focused on monetization -- turning a profit in streaming is easier said than done.

Then there’s the macro picture. Advertising remains weak, consumer spending is wobbly, and Hollywood is still navigating the aftershocks of last year’s strikes. Any hiccup in content output or delays in the monetization strategy could set the turnaround back.

And lastly, this is a complex merger. Integrations are messy. Culture clashes and regulatory second-guessing could hurt. None of that is in the base case, but you need to be eyes wide open.

Still a BUY

This stock is still a BUY -- just like I argued in my original piece a year ago. Back then, I called PARA a modern value play trapped under legacy leadership. Today, that leadership is gone. The FCC has cleared the runway, new capital is in, new management is in place, and the business is finally being reshaped with a clear sense of urgency.

Yes, there are risks, but the upside now feels more tangible. Pluto is being prioritized, costs are being cut, the balance sheet is healing, and there’s a real team in place with every incentive to get the stock to $30 and beyond.
